definition: Annual Equivalent Rate. The interest rate on a savings account if interest were paid and compounded once a year. Useful for comparing savings accounts with different interest payment frequencies.
A statement from a mortgage lender indicating how much they would likely lend you, subject to a full application. Also called a Decision in Principle (DIP) or Mortgage in Principle.
The maximum amount you can contribute to pensions each year while still receiving tax relief. Currently £60,000 or 100% of earnings, whichever is lower.
A retirement product that converts your pension pot into a guaranteed income for life. Once purchased, the terms are typically fixed and cannot be changed.
Annual Percentage Rate. The total cost of borrowing over a year, including interest and fees. Used to compare loans and credit cards. The higher the APR, the more expensive the borrowing.
When you owe money that should have already been paid. Being in mortgage arrears means you've missed one or more mortgage payments.
The interest rate set by the Bank of England that influences borrowing and savings rates across the UK economy. Changes to the base rate affect mortgage rates and savings returns.
Loans to governments or companies that pay regular interest and return your capital at a set date. Generally considered lower risk than shares but higher risk than cash savings.
A short-term loan used to bridge a gap in financing, often when buying a new property before selling an existing one. Typically has higher interest rates than standard mortgages.
Tax on the profit when you sell an asset that has increased in value. Applies to property (except main residence), shares, and other investments above your annual allowance.
A tax-free savings account where interest earned is exempt from income tax. Part of your annual ISA allowance.
A sequence of linked property transactions where each sale depends on the completion of another. Chains can cause delays if one transaction falls through.
The final stage of a property purchase when ownership legally transfers, funds are exchanged, and you receive the keys.
Interest calculated on both the initial principal and accumulated interest from previous periods. Over time, this creates exponential growth in savings or debt.
The legal process of transferring property ownership from seller to buyer. Usually handled by a solicitor or licensed conveyancer.
A numerical representation of your creditworthiness based on your credit history. Lenders use it to assess risk when you apply for credit. Higher scores generally mean better rates.
A pension scheme that pays a guaranteed income in retirement based on salary and years of service. Also called a final salary pension. Increasingly rare in the private sector.
A pension scheme where you and/or your employer contribute to a pot that's invested. Your retirement income depends on contributions made and investment performance.
The upfront payment you make when buying a property, expressed as a percentage of the purchase price. Larger deposits typically secure better mortgage rates.
A payment made by a company to its shareholders from its profits. Can provide regular income from share investments.
A flexible way to access pension savings in retirement. You can take lump sums or regular amounts while leaving the rest invested. Also called flexi-access drawdown.
A fee charged by mortgage lenders if you pay off your mortgage or overpay beyond limits during a fixed or discounted rate period.
An investment policy once used to repay interest-only mortgages. Many failed to provide enough to clear the debt, causing an 'endowment gap'.
The portion of your property you own outright—the difference between its value and any outstanding mortgage. Builds as you repay the mortgage or if property values rise.
Ways for homeowners (usually over 55) to access the value tied up in their home without selling. Includes lifetime mortgages and home reversion plans.
The point in a property transaction when the sale becomes legally binding. Both buyer and seller are committed, and pulling out would incur significant costs.
Financial Conduct Authority. The UK regulator responsible for consumer protection in financial services and maintaining market integrity.
Someone who has never owned a property before. First-time buyers may qualify for stamp duty relief and certain government schemes.
A mortgage where the interest rate stays the same for a set period (typically 2-5 years), regardless of changes to the base rate. Provides payment certainty.
Outright ownership of a property and the land it sits on, with no time limit. Compare with leasehold.
Financial Services Compensation Scheme. Protects UK consumers if a financial firm fails. Covers up to £85,000 per person per banking institution for deposits.
A pooled investment that holds a collection of assets like shares or bonds. Allows diversification without needing to buy individual holdings.
When a seller accepts a higher offer from another buyer after already accepting yours. Legal in England and Wales until exchange of contracts.
When a buyer lowers their offer just before exchange of contracts, knowing the seller may feel pressured to accept.
An annual fee paid by leaseholders to the freeholder. Can increase over time depending on lease terms. Recent legislation limits ground rent on new leases.
Government schemes designed to help first-time buyers and home movers purchase a property. Various versions have existed; check current availability.
His Majesty's Revenue and Customs. The UK government department responsible for collecting taxes and administering benefits.
Tax paid on earned income including wages, self-employment profits, and some savings interest. Rates vary by income band: basic (20%), higher (40%), additional (45%).
A type of fund that tracks a specific market index (like the FTSE 100) rather than trying to beat it. Generally has lower fees than actively managed funds.
The rate at which prices increase over time, reducing purchasing power. The Bank of England targets 2% inflation. Measured by CPI (Consumer Prices Index).
Tax on the estate of someone who has died, currently 40% on amounts above the nil-rate band (£325,000). Various exemptions and reliefs can apply.
A mortgage where monthly payments cover only the interest, not the capital. The full loan amount must be repaid at the end of the term.
Individual Savings Account. A tax-efficient wrapper for savings or investments where gains and income are tax-free. Annual allowance is currently £20,000.
Government department that registers ownership of land and property in England and Wales. Records are public and can be searched for a fee.
Ownership of a property for a fixed period, as specified in a lease with the freeholder. Common for flats. Lease length affects value and mortgageability.
Previously the maximum amount you could hold in pensions without additional tax charges. Abolished from April 2024, though some protections continue.
An ISA for under-40s saving for a first home or retirement. Government adds 25% bonus on contributions up to £4,000 per year. Penalties apply for other withdrawals.
Your mortgage amount as a percentage of the property value. A £180,000 mortgage on a £200,000 property is 90% LTV. Lower LTV typically means better rates.
A loan secured against property. If you fail to keep up repayments, the lender can repossess the property to recover their money.
Contributions deducted from earnings that count towards State Pension and certain benefits. Different classes apply to employed and self-employed individuals.
When your property is worth less than the outstanding mortgage. Makes it difficult to sell or remortgage.
The threshold below which no Inheritance Tax is charged on an estate. Currently £325,000, with an additional residence nil-rate band for homes left to direct descendants.
A mortgage linked to savings accounts where your savings balance reduces the mortgage amount on which interest is charged.
Paying more than your required mortgage payment to reduce the outstanding balance faster. Check for early repayment charges and overpayment limits.
Pay As You Earn. The system by which employers deduct income tax and National Insurance from employee wages before payment.
A long-term savings plan for retirement with tax advantages. Types include workplace pensions, personal pensions, and the State Pension.
The amount of income you can earn each year before paying income tax. Currently £12,570. Reduces for those earning over £100,000.
A savings product from NS&I where instead of earning interest, you're entered into a monthly prize draw. Capital is secure but returns aren't guaranteed.
Switching your mortgage to a new deal, either with your current lender or a different one. Often done when a fixed rate period ends.
A mortgage where monthly payments cover both interest and capital, so the loan is fully repaid by the end of the term. Also called a capital repayment mortgage.
The system for reporting income and paying tax if you're self-employed, have additional income, or need to claim tax relief. Returns due by 31 January.
Fees paid by leaseholders towards the maintenance of communal areas and building services. Can vary significantly between properties.
A scheme where you buy a share of a property (25-75%) and pay rent on the rest. You can usually increase your share over time ('staircasing').
Self-Invested Personal Pension. A pension wrapper that gives you control over investment choices. Often has a wider range of investments than workplace pensions.
A qualified legal professional who handles conveyancing, contracts, and other legal matters in property transactions.
Tax paid when buying property in England and Northern Ireland above certain thresholds. Rates increase with property value. First-time buyers have relief.
The default interest rate your mortgage moves to after a fixed or discounted period ends. Usually higher than available fixed rates.
Regular payment from the government in retirement. Requires minimum National Insurance contributions. Full new State Pension requires 35 qualifying years.
A tax-efficient wrapper for investments in shares, funds, and bonds. Any gains or income are free from capital gains tax and income tax.
An inspection of a property's condition before purchase. Types range from basic valuations to full structural surveys. Can reveal issues affecting value or safety.
A code used by employers to calculate how much income tax to deduct. Usually starts with your Personal Allowance divided by 10.
Reduction in tax owed based on allowable expenses or contributions. Pension contributions receive tax relief at your marginal rate.
The UK tax year runs from 6 April to 5 April the following year. Allowances reset at the start of each tax year.
A mortgage with an interest rate that moves in line with the Bank of England base rate, typically set at base rate plus a fixed percentage.
A legal arrangement where assets are held by trustees for the benefit of beneficiaries. Used for estate planning, protecting assets, and managing money for others.
The process lenders use to assess your application and decide whether to approve it and on what terms.
An assessment of a property's market value. Mortgage lenders require a basic valuation; buyers may commission more detailed surveys.
A mortgage where the interest rate can change during the term. Includes tracker mortgages, discount mortgages, and SVRs.
A pension scheme arranged by your employer. Auto-enrolment means most employees are automatically enrolled and receive employer contributions.
The income return on an investment, expressed as a percentage. For property, rental yield is annual rent divided by property value.
